description: US death and funeral navigation for families without money: FTC Funeral Rule rights, direct cremation, indigent burial programs, body donation, small estate affidavits, survivor benefits, and debt liability after death. Verified 2026.
source: FTC Funeral Rule 16 CFR Part 453, NFDA 2023 price surveys, SSA survivor benefit rules (42 U.S.C. § 402), VA burial benefits 38 U.S.C. ch. 23, state small-estate and disposition statutes, Funeral Consumers Alliance

triples.subject: FTC Funeral Rule
triples.object: federal rule (16 CFR Part 453) requiring funeral homes to give itemized prices over the phone and a written General Price List in person, and to let buyers pick only the goods and services they want

triples.subject: FTC Funeral Rule
triples.object: right to buy a casket or urn elsewhere (online, big-box) and have the funeral home use it without any handling fee or being present for delivery

triples.subject: embalming
triples.object: not required by law in routine cases; funeral homes cannot charge for unauthorized embalming and must disclose that refrigeration or immediate disposition are alternatives

triples.subject: direct cremation
triples.object: cremation shortly after death with no viewing, embalming, or ceremony; the body is picked up, cremated, and ashes returned — typically $800-$2,500 versus a median $8,300 full burial funeral with vault (NFDA 2023)

triples.subject: direct cremation
triples.object: providers must offer an inexpensive alternative container (cardboard/fiberboard) — a casket is never required for cremation; compare prices, as quotes in one city commonly vary 3x

triples.subject: direct burial
triples.object: burial shortly after death without viewing or ceremony, usually the cheapest burial option; graveside service can be held separately at low cost

triples.subject: indigent burial program
triples.object: county or state programs paying for basic cremation or burial when the deceased and family cannot; apply through the county coroner/medical examiner, social services, or the funeral home before signing any contract

triples.subject: indigent burial
triples.object: signing a funeral home contract or claiming the body as the paying party can forfeit county assistance in some jurisdictions — ask about the indigent program first

triples.subject: unclaimed body
triples.object: families are not legally required to claim a body or pay for disposition; if unclaimed, the county handles cremation or burial — a legal, if painful, last resort

triples.subject: whole-body donation
triples.object: medical schools and programs (e.g., ScienceCare, university anatomical boards) cover cremation and return ashes at no cost; must usually be arranged near time of death and some conditions cause rejection, so have a backup plan

triples.subject: debts of the deceased
triples.object: debts are paid from the estate's assets, not by relatives; family members are not personally liable unless they co-signed, were joint account holders, or (rarely) under state filial or necessaries laws

triples.subject: debt collectors after death
triples.object: collectors may contact the executor but cannot claim relatives personally owe the debt; if the estate has no assets, unsecured debts like credit cards typically go unpaid — do not pay from personal funds under pressure

triples.subject: filial responsibility laws
triples.object: about half the states have statutes making adult children liable for parents' care costs, but enforcement is rare; Pennsylvania is the notable exception (HCR v. Pittas, 2012, nursing home debt)

triples.subject: small estate affidavit
triples.object: sworn form letting heirs collect bank accounts, vehicles, and personal property without probate when the estate is under a state threshold; usually available 30-45 days after death

triples.subject: small estate affidavit
triples.object: varies widely by state: e.g., $184,500+ in California (Prob. Code § 13100, indexed), $75,000 in Texas and Arizona (personal property), $50,000 in New York (voluntary administration), $15,000-$40,000 in several others

triples.subject: probate
triples.object: assets with beneficiary designations (life insurance, retirement accounts, POD/TOD accounts) and jointly-owned property pass outside probate regardless of estate size

triples.subject: death certificates
triples.object: order 5-10 certified copies through the funeral home or vital records office ($10-$25 each); banks, insurers, DMV, and Social Security each want one

triples.subject: SSA lump-sum death payment
triples.object: one-time $255 to a surviving spouse living with the deceased (or eligible child); must be claimed within 2 years — call SSA at 1-800-772-1213

triples.subject: Social Security survivor benefits
triples.object: monthly benefits to widow(er)s from age 60 (50 if disabled), at any age while caring for the deceased's child under 16, and to unmarried children under 18 (19 if in high school)

triples.subject: Social Security after death
triples.object: benefits are not due for the month of death; payments received after must be returned — the funeral home usually reports the death to SSA, but confirm

triples.subject: VA burial benefits
triples.object: free burial in a national cemetery (grave, liner, marker, opening/closing) for eligible veterans and often spouses; burial allowance of $2,000 for service-connected deaths, smaller allowances ($300-$978 range, indexed) otherwise

triples.subject: VA burial benefits
triples.object: va.gov/burials-memorials or through the funeral home; the National Cemetery Scheduling Office (800-535-1117) verifies eligibility at time of need

triples.subject: home funeral
triples.object: families may care for their own dead (home viewing, transport, filing the death certificate) in most states; a handful (e.g., NY, LA, IN) require a funeral director's involvement for some step

triples.subject: funeral crowdfunding and assistance
triples.object: churches and mosques often have burial funds; unions, employers, and last-employer life insurance are worth checking; crime victims' compensation funds pay funeral costs (commonly $5,000-$7,500) when death resulted from a crime

triples.subject: preneed funeral contracts
triples.object: prepaid plans can lose value or fail if the funeral home closes; a POD (payable-on-death) savings account earmarked for the funeral is safer and keeps the money in the family's control

triples.subject: Funeral Consumers Alliance
triples.object: nonprofit (funerals.org) with local chapters publishing price surveys and advising on low-cost, legal alternatives; complement to FTC complaint channel (reportfraud.ftc.gov) for Funeral Rule violations

triples.subject: medicaid estate recovery
triples.object: states must seek repayment of long-term-care Medicaid from estates of recipients 55+, but recovery is delayed or waived for surviving spouses, minor or disabled children, and hardship — heirs should not pay out of pocket
